title: Fitting reflective foil behind external-wall radiators
subtitle: Cutting heat lost through the wall behind a radiator
excerpt: Radiators mounted on external walls lose 20 to 25 percent of their heat backwards through the wall rather than into the room. A reflective foil panel fitted behind each radiator redirects that heat back into the room. The fix costs under 5 pounds per radiator and takes about ten minutes.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust guidance on low-cost insulation measures (2024). CIBSE Guide A heat loss through external walls. British Board of Agrément assessment notes on reflective radiator foil products.

glossaryTerms.definition: A point or linear element in a building envelope that conducts heat more readily than the surrounding material, causing a local cold spot and increased heat loss. Window frames, wall ties, and uninsulated joist ends are common thermal bridges.

content.text: A radiator on an external wall heats the wall as well as the room. The wall conducts heat outward. A reflective foil panel behind the radiator reduces that loss by reflecting infrared radiation back into the room. Studies suggest a well-fitted panel reduces heat loss through that section of wall by 45 percent or more. At current UK gas prices of 6p/kWh, each panel saves roughly 5 to 10 pounds per heating season.
External walls with no cavity wall insulation gain the most. If your cavity is already filled, the saving is smaller but still present. Internal walls do not benefit at all.
What you need
Reflective radiator foil (sold by the roll or as pre-cut panels). Look for a product with a reflective surface rated above 95 percent emissivity.
Tape measure.
Utility knife or scissors.
Double-sided tape or panel adhesive strips (some products include these).
Installation
Turn the radiator off and let it cool. This makes handling easier and avoids burns.
Measure the height and width of the wall section behind the radiator. Cut the foil panel to match, with the reflective side facing outward (toward the radiator, away from the wall).

Slide the panel behind the radiator, positioning it flush against the wall. The top edge should sit at or above the radiator's top rail. The panel must not obstruct the gap between the radiator and the wall, which needs to stay open for convection.
Secure the panel with double-sided tape if it will not stay in place on its own. Tape along the top edge only so the panel can be removed if needed.

Turn the radiator back on and run the heating for 30 minutes. The panel should feel warm but not hot on the room-facing side. If the panel is very hot, it is too close to the radiator and needs repositioning.
What done well looks like

The panel sits flat against the wall with the reflective surface facing the radiator. There is a clear gap between panel and radiator. The room heats up at the same rate as before but the wall behind the radiator stays closer to room temperature. A
thermal bridging
check with a thermal camera before and after will show the wall temperature change clearly.
What done badly looks like

The panel is pressed hard against the radiator, blocking convection. The room takes longer to heat up. The panel has slipped down and no longer covers the full wall area. The wrong side is facing out: the plain side faces the radiator and the shiny side faces the wall, doing nothing useful.
Troubleshooter

attrs.cause: No fixing — the gap behind the radiator is too wide for friction alone
attrs.fix: Apply double-sided tape along the top edge of the panel to the wall. Check the tape is rated for mild heat.

attrs.symptom: Radiator takes longer to heat up after fitting
attrs.cause: Panel is touching the radiator body and blocking convection
attrs.fix: Reposition so there is at least 20 mm of clear air gap between panel and radiator.

attrs.symptom: No visible effect on room temperature
attrs.cause: Cavity wall insulation already fitted, or radiator is on an internal wall
attrs.fix: Check the wall type first. Internal wall radiators do not benefit from reflective foil.
